title: AI and the Human Voice
body:
Sometimes, I do workshops with teams about writing and working in the open. Those workshops often include a bit about writing bad first drafts, and how it's good to knock out lots of drafts frequently - they help you think, and they help teams work out what they want to share, and how.

But of course, drafting things is something that AI tools are supposed to be good at. Here's something someone said to me in a recent workshop:

"Why should we follow your advice when we're constantly being told by senior management to make more use of AI? Writing first drafts is exactly the sort of task I'd ask CoPilot to do."

So, yes, fair point. Here's my response to that.

First of all, I'll come clean about where I stand, generally speaking: I'm an AI sceptic, especially on using AI for writing. I can see it being useful for other things - but that's because I'm a writer, right?

I see AI generated text and most of the time, I think it's rubbish. It's dull, it's derivative, it always sounds like a thousand other things I've read before. Because the AI has been trained on those thousands of things, all now easy to find on the internet.

But: do I think AI is quite good at making simple software, or basic web tools? Well, yeah, I have tried it for that, and I thought: "Hmm yeah this isn't too shabby."

And of course I would think that, wouldn't I? I don't know better. I'm not a software engineer.

I have a feeling that everyone likes using AI tools to try doing someone else's profession. They're much less keen when someone else uses it for their profession. I fall into the same trap as everyone else. I recognise, and admit to, my own bias.

So, bias in hand, allow to me to plough ahead with some opinions.

AI thinks good writing is full of cliches

I think AI is a terrible writer. Lots of people disagree with me, especially people who aren't professional writers. So, let me try and prove my point.

Have a look at tropes.md. It's a Markdown config file you can use to instruct your AI tool to avoid doing certain things when it drafts text for you.

It has lots of advice for AI bots:

Don't use "rich tapestry" or "complex landscape" or "pivotal moment" or "it's worth noting" or (this is one my faves) "laser focus"

Don't use "XYZ serves as ABC" or "it's not X - it's Y" etc etc

Look carefully at what tropes.md is telling AI tools to avoid ... what's the simple way of summing up its advice?

CLICHES. It's a list of cliches. And tropes.md is just formalising the advice: "Avoid cliches." This is age-old advice that's been around for almost as long as writing.

But AIs and LLMs write in cliches because they were trained on the internet, and the internet is 76% cliches.

This is hard for non-professional writers to spot, because cliches feel normal. After all, we all use them verbally, every day:

It's raining cats and dogs Let's take this offline Think outside the box

This is why AI writing makes me roll my eyes. If a junior writer sent me a draft with that many cliches in it, I'd ask them to write it again without them. It's the cliches that make AI text sound so derivative.

The twist is: sometimes cliches are ok

Here's another thing that professional writers will tell you: not all cliches are all bad, all the time. Sometimes it's good to use a cliche. Sometimes it helps you make a point, or express something with more clarity. Human writers learn how and when to add cliches - they know how to dial the "clicheness" up and down responsibly. Human writers have, or can learn, how to use language with subtlety.

AI writing tools can't do that.

When I'm doing my workshops with teams, the main advice I give for anyone wanting to make their text more approachable - wanting to actually use the human voice - is to add "tiny sprinklings of poetry".

They're so tiny, most people won't notice them. That's the point. They don't dominate, but they are additive. They add something to the whole. They're the flourishes that we automatically add when we're talking, but few of us remember to mix in to our writing.

I'd argue: they humanise text that would otherwise be as dry as a stick.

A little alliteration. A gentle pun. A joke. A witty sub-heading. A subtle callback to a point made earlier - perhaps by reusing the same words, but in a different order. A playful bit of punctuation. Deliberately bad sentences. Repetition.

Adding just tiny sprinkling of some of these to a piece of text will make it sound more like speech, be easier to read, and be more memorable afterwards.

AI can write poems, but it ain't no poet

Thing is, you can ask an LLM to write a poem about anything, and it'll do it. Any style you like. Any subject matter.

But in most work-related circumstances, we don't want poems. The tiny sprinklings of poetry are helpful, but no-one's looking for a corporate strategy plan in rhyming couplets.

AI can't do subtle playful flourishes in text, because it has no sense of subtlety. AI goes all in: either it writes you a poem, or it doesn't.

It cannot figure out how to be subtly poetic in tiny doses, where appropriate. It doesn't know what "appropriate" is. But you do. You know it when you read it. It makes you smile, or raise an eyebrow, or make a note, or pass on to a friend.

Human writing needs human input

So, sure, ok: if you want to, or if your boss is insisting on it, go ahead and use an AI tool to write something for you. Perhaps feed tropes.md into it, and see if that helps. Maybe it will.

But think about your goal: you want humans to read the text.

To get people to pay attention to it, to take it in, to remember what it says: you're going to have to study your AI's first draft with a particularly careful eye. Apply your sense of the poetic. Add some tiny sprinkles where they make sense. Do the work to make it sound human, by adding a few of the flourishes you'd add without thinking if you were speaking to your audience directly. Get a buddy check from a human colleague, not from another AI. Steer the words towards humanity by steering them towards humans.
